title: Generative AI for Solutions Architects
description:
## Designing Safe GenAI Workloads on AWS

The SAP-C02 guide flags **generative and agentic AI** as an emerging area. As an architect you are expected to design **security and responsible-AI controls** around foundation models — not to train them.

### The core building blocks

| Service | Role |
|---|---|
| **Amazon Bedrock** | Managed access to foundation models (FMs) via a single API; no model hosting to manage. |
| **Bedrock Guardrails** | Policy layer on top of FMs: filter harmful content, block **denied topics**, and **redact/deny PII** — applied consistently regardless of the underlying model. |
| **Bedrock AgentCore Identity** | Identity and **scoped access control** for **agentic** AI applications (agents that call tools/APIs on a user's behalf), so an agent acts with least privilege. |
| **AWS Step Functions** | Orchestrates **human-in-the-loop** approval workflows — pause an AI-initiated action until a person approves (task token / callback pattern). |

### Responsible-AI design principles

- **Guardrails at the boundary** — enforce content and PII policy on both prompts and responses, decoupled from the model choice.
- **Least-privilege agents** — give agentic apps a scoped identity (AgentCore Identity) rather than broad, shared credentials.
- **Human oversight** — insert approval gates (Step Functions) before high-impact or irreversible AI actions.
- **Auditability & privacy** — enable model-invocation logging; Bedrock does **not** use your prompts/outputs to train the base models.

These controls combine into a defense-in-depth pattern: scoped identity → guardrails → human approval → audit log. Mark complete, then work through the emerging-topic questions.
